truth.papers.A6.title: Research status and development prospect of FeeMneCeAl system low-density steels
truth.papers.A6.paper_scope: This review systematically summarizes the composition design, production processes, strengthening and strain hardening mechanisms, microstructure evolution, and service performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, focusing on solid solution strengthening, grain refinement, precipitation strengthening, TRIP/TWIP effects, and other deformation mechanisms.

prediction.papers.A6.title: Research status and development prospect of Fe-Mn-C-Al system low-density steels
prediction.papers.A6.paper_scope: Systematic review of composition design, production processes, strengthening and strain hardening mechanisms, microstructure evolution, and service performance (cryogenic, impact toughness, fatigue, hydrogen embrittlement, wear, and tensile properties) of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels.

prediction.processes.proc_fe22.5mn1.2al0.3c_01.desc: Cold rolling with 30%, 40% or 50% reduction and subsequent annealing
prediction.processes.proc_fe22.5mn1.2al0.3c_01.proc: Annealing temperatures 550°C and 630°C; annealing times from 0.1 s to 100000 s.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_fe22.5mn1.2al0.3: Cold rolling reductions of 30%, 40% and 50%; annealing at 550°C or 630°C for times ranging from 0.1 s to 100000 s.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The density of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is estimated by ρ = 8.15 - 0.101 ω(Al) - 0.41 ω(C) - 0.0085 ω(Mn) (in g/cm³).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Austenite lattice constant is given by α = 0.35721 + 0.0002771 ω(Al) + 0.0044089 ω(C) + 0.0001166 ω(Mn) nm.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Yield strength increments from alloying: 10.5 MPa per wt% Al, 0.58 MPa per wt% Mn, 180.7 MPa per wt% C.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: For Fe-xMn-2Al-0.45C low-density steels at cryogenic temperature, Charpy impact energy drops from 80.5 J (17Mn) to 41.1 J (22Mn).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: In Fe-18Mn-xAl-0.6C steels, cryogenic impact energy increases from 122 J at 3 wt% Al to 138 J at 5 wt% Al, then decreases at 8 wt% Al.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Single austenite Fe-30Mn-11Al-1.2C steel shows 1058 MPa yield strength, 1310 MPa UTS, and 38% total elongation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: The relationship between SFE and deformation mechanism: TRIP at low SFE, TWIP at moderate, MBIP/SIP/DSBR at high SFE.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fig. 19 shows the 'banana curve' of strength vs. ductility for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, indicating single austenite and A-BDP steels have superior combination.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Fig. 13a: Charpy impact energy of Fe-xMn-2Al-0.45C-0.05Si as a function of Mn content at -196 °C and 0 °C.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Fig. 13b: Charpy impact energy of Fe-18Mn-xAl-0.6C as a function of Al content.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: The density of Fe-Mn-Al-C steel is determined by alloying composition per Eq. (1): ρ = 8.15 - 0.101ω(Al) - 0.41ω(C) - 0.0085ω(Mn). C plays the most significant role in reducing density, but density is mainly controlled by Al content because Al density (2.7 g/cm3) is much lower than Mn density (7.2 g/cm3).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Austenite lattice constant variation follows Eq. (2): α = 0.35721 + 0.0002771ω(Al) + 0.0044089ω(C) + 0.0001166ω(Mn). C atom is most important for expanding austenite lattice constant because it forms interstitial solid solutions, while Al and Mn form substitutional solid solutions.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Yield strength increment equations for austenite: σ_yield = 10.5ω(Al) + 351.5; σ_yield = 0.58ω(Mn) + 328.0; σ_yield = 180.7ω(C) + 250.7. C and Al play a more positive role in yield strength increment than Mn.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Microstructure classification of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels after hot rolling and rapid cooling: single ferrite steel (XMn<5.0, 5.0<XAl<12.0, XC<0.1; phases δ, B2, DO3, κ), single austenite steel (15.0<XMn<30.0, 2.0<XAl<12.0, 0.5<XC<2.0; phases γ, α, δ, κ, β-Mn), A-BDP steel (10.0<XMn<25.0, 5.0<XAl<12.0, 0.6<XC<1.0; phases α, B2, DO3, γ, κ, β-Mn), F-BDP steel (3.0<XMn<10.0, 3.0<XAl<9.0, XC<0.4; phases α, δ, B2, DO3, γ, κ, α').
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: κ-carbide precipitates in three modes: intergranular κ*-carbide along grain boundaries, intragranular κ0-carbide within grains, and simultaneous precipitation along grain boundaries and within grains (Fig. 2).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: In single ferrite Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, δ-ferrite presents banded morphology along the rolling direction under hot rolling because high Al content increases ferrite recrystallization temperature and limits dynamic/static recrystallization.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Single austenite Fe-Mn-Al-C steels exhibit equiaxed austenite with annealing twins at room temperature; after cold rolling, grains elongate into lath-like morphology along the rolling direction.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Typical thermomechanical processing: forging ingot homogenized at 1100–1250°C for 1–3 h, then hot-rolled to 2–5 mm thickness between 1100 and 850°C; aging at 500–700°C for 0.5–2 h benefits nano precipitate nucleation (Fig. 3).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Zhang et al. [48] designed an ultrafine-grained A-BDP steel (Fe-25.7Mn-10.6Al-1.2C) via austenite recrystallization and ferrite transformation; the final average grain size reached 1.12 ± 0.57 μm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: Chen et al. [50] studied aging of Fe-30Mn-10Al-2C at 500°C, 600°C and 700°C: at 500°C κ-carbide precipitated along austenite grain boundaries; at 600°C β-Mn generated with dramatic secondary hardening; at 700°C β-Mn along grain boundary played an inactive role in ductility.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_10.value: Liu et al. [51] investigated aging time (3, 10, 30 and 720 min) in Fe-11Mn-10Al-1.2C at 500°C: continuous eutectoid reaction increased nano κ-carbide fraction and yield strength after 720 min.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_11.value: Solution strengthening contributions in austenite (Fig. 5a): 1 wt% C contributes 187–300 MPa yield strength increment, while 1 wt% Al contributes slightly below 10 MPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_12.value: Grain refinement strengthening in Fe-Mn-Al-C dual-phase steel (Fig. 5b): austenite grain refinement increased yield strength and ultimate tensile strength above 400 MPa.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_13.value: κ-carbide has L12 ordered crystal structure similar to perovskite oxide; chemical formula (Fe,Mn)yAl1-yCx (-0.2<y<+0.2, 0.42<x<0.71). High C (>0.7 wt%) and Al (>7.0 wt%) are primary conditions for κ-carbide formation in single austenite-base steels.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_14.value: B2 phase is CsCl-type ordered BCC; three morphologies (banded, blocky, ultrafine) observed in aged Fe-16Mn-9.6Al-0.86C-4.9Ni steel [71]. Ni addition promotes NiAl-type B2 formation. B2 phase is not sheared by slip dislocations, causing dislocation pileup at phase boundary and enhancing work hardening.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_15.value: MC carbide strengthening by Nb, V, Ti additions: 0.2 wt% Nb in Fe-8Al-5Mn-0.1C steel resulted in 0.2 vol% NbC versus 2.3 vol% κ-carbide, refining austenite grains and increasing yield strength by about 100 MPa without deteriorating ductility [59].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_16.value: Deformation mechanisms in single austenite steels depend on stacking fault energy (SFE): TRIP at low SFE, TWIP at medium SFE, MBIP/SIP/DSBR at high SFE (Fig. 7, Table 3).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_17.value: Frommeyer et al. [85] studied Fe-28Mn-10Al-1.2C steel (SFE 110 mJ/m2): deformation twins were almost restrained, shear bands formed with dislocation slips, nano κ-carbides uniformly arranged, and SIP effect was beneficial to total elongation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_18.value: Hong et al. [86] studied Fe-17.5Mn-1.5Al-0.85C TWIP steel: deformation twins nucleated at prior austenite grain boundaries when tensile strain reached 0.02, and grew into grains by strain 0.05.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_19.value: Park et al. [87] proposed MBIP in quenched Fe-28Mn-xAl-(0.8–1.0)C steel: microbands formed due to interaction between mobile dislocations and Taylor crystal lattices, leading to 873 MPa tensile strength and 100% total elongation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_20.value: Welsch et al. [88] studied Fe-30.4Mn-8Al-1.2C steel (SFE 85 mJ/m2): DSBR was the main strain hardening mechanism, giving 900 MPa tensile strength and 68% total elongation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_21.value: A-BDP steels show multi-stage strain hardening due to hardness difference between ferrite and austenite; Fe-12Mn-8Al-0.8C showed three stages of ln(dσ/dε) vs. lnσ curve (Fig. 10) [90].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_22.value: Park et al. [91] investigated Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.8C A-BDP steel with 27% δ-ferrite: coarse B2 and ultrafine DO3 precipitated in ferrite; strain hardening controlled by super-dislocations shearing ordered phases in ferrite and plane slip dislocations in austenite.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_23.value: F-BDP steels (e.g., Fe-3.9Mn-5.8Al-0.35C) exhibit five stages of strain hardening involving dislocation motion in ferrite, limited TRIP/TWIP at low strain, and rapid martensite transformation at high strain (Fig. 12) [15].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_24.value: Austenite stability factors include grain size, C content in austenite, constraining effect from adjacent phases, and austenite volume fraction, though disputes remain [94–100].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_25.value: Cryogenic performance: Lee et al. [101] reported Fe-xMn-2Al-0.45C steels (x=17,19,22) showed decreased ductility and impact energy with increasing Mn content at cryogenic temperature; cryogenic ductility values were 75.7%, 53.4%, 9.7% and Charpy impact energies were 80.5 J, 49.8 J, 41.1 J respectively.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_26.value: Chen et al. [102] investigated Fe-18Mn-xAl-0.6C steels: cryogenic impact energy increased from 122 J (3% Al) to 138 J (5% Al), then decreased at 8% Al. Strain-induced martensite transformation in Fe-18Mn-8Al-0.6C relieved local stress and enhanced impact energy.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_27.value: Impact toughness of aged Fe-Mn-Al-C A-BDP steels significantly decreased at room temperature after aging at 550°C for 16 h; Fe-30Mn-10Al-1C steel impact energy approached 0 J due to κ-carbide precipitation (Fig. 15a).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_28.value: Al content affects impact toughness of aged steels (Fig. 15b): <7% Al may form Fe3C7 and M7C3 along grain boundaries; 7–10% Al precipitates brittle κ-carbide at grain boundaries; >10% Al forms δ-ferrite reducing toughness due to deformation incompatibility.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_29.value: Fatigue life of Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.86C austenite steel was about twice that of Fe-12Cr-1.5Ni-0.2V-1.8W-0.5Mo-0.15C martensite stainless steel [107]; fatigue crack growth resistance stronger than 304L stainless steel [108].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_30.value: Hamada et al. [109] found decreased grain size enhanced fatigue resistance in high-Mn steels; cracks preferentially generated at intersections of slip bands, grain boundaries, and annealing twin boundaries. Micro-alloyed Fe-Mn-Al-C steels exhibited higher fatigue life than non-microalloyed ones [111].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_31.value: Hydrogen embrittlement in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels: fracture modes mainly intergranular and transgranular; transgranular fracture initiated parallel to deformation twin boundaries [112]. In Fe-26Mn-11Al-1.2C, grain boundary triple junction cracking and slip-localization-induced intergranular cracking along micro-voids determined embrittlement [113].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_32.value: Hydrogen embrittlement is closely related to κ-carbide particles. For high C (>1 wt%) steels, H embrittlement occurred in interdendritic area due to κ-carbide; for low C (<0.05 wt%) steels, in DO3 phase [114]. κ-carbide/austenite interface acts as an effective hydrogen capture center [115].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_33.value: Wear performance: Fe-25.1Mn-6.6Al-1.3C steel quenched at 1050°C and aged at 550°C for 2 h showed best wear resistance (2.66 g−1) due to fine nano-size κ-carbide; longer aging to 4 h caused over-coarsening and brittleness, decreasing wear resistance to 1.92 g−1 (Fig. 18e) [117].
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_34.value: Fig. 19 shows room-temperature tensile properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels with various Mn contents follow a banana curve; single austenite and A-BDP steels appear superior. Fe-30Mn-11Al-1.2C shows 1058 MPa yield strength, 1310 MPa tensile strength, and 38% total elongation [137]. High Mn content expands space for enhancing strength and ductility but increases material cost and processing difficulty.
